speaker: interviewer
text: Clara Skaletzky, on January 24, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished there with where I was, in the attic, with my mother. And my-- my sister came up. Because the place where she was sit-- staying there, the woman says she cannot keep her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came back to [INAUDIBLE], to [INAUDIBLE]. She know-- knew that I am there. And she was talking to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you can go to see your sister at night. But I cannot keep you. I cannot keep two people. I keep only one. If you want to stay here, you have to go, both of-- both of you have to go. And he didn't know that I am there. My mother was there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came up, and she start crying. And all night, we stopped talking. My mother picked herself up with the-- with my sister and left me there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was gone-- it was really cold already. It was winter, December, November, the end of November already. '42, November '42. Then my mother said I could-- she couldn't-- she didn't want to go see me no more. But in down-- downstairs in the-- in the-- in the barn, she used to come up. And she-- we'd-- I'd talk to her. She says I have to come up and talk to you. So come downstairs to the barn. And there is some-- some cracks between the-- between the wood. And look me up there. And I'll talk to you. I'll tell you what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every evening I used to go down and look through the cracks, to see my mother. I-- and I used to spare food, whatever she bring-- the-- the woman gave me, the Tedoch-- Mrs. Tedoch give me. And I hand it over to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because maybe she was hungry. I give-- I couldn't give her liquid food. But I left her the bread, or you know, everything, [? solid. ?] Through this-- the-- the cracks was kind of big. I could stick it through the cracks. Then one-- one day she came up and says-- she came up to me. And I talk to her. She says, listen, Chaia, I think we're not going to live through. Maybe you're going to live through. The food is not important. It's very cold now. We cannot-- we cannot take it no more. I don't have place for it to go. And you know, Rachel, I'm staying-- I'm going around with Rachel and we are both almost dying from frost, from-- from-- from the-- from the-- from the cold. I was crying. I cried with her all night. She cried, and I cried through the-- through the cracks. I couldn't reach her hand, because it was too small, the crack. I just pushed through the food, and she left. Laying over there, by the-- by the-- and the attic over there And there-- in the-- the cold. It was cold, too. But I had to stay there, in this cold. You cannot-- you cannot be in the house. I covered myself up with the hay. It's warm. I mean, the hay is not so bad. This is all-- all up to December, the end, I didn't hear from my mother when the holiday, Christmas, came up. When Christmas came up, my mother came up to the Mr. Tedoch. And I was-- I-- they are already downstairs. He had a little-- like, a little room he used to-- used-- he was a tanner. He used to soak the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the skins from the animals, you know, to-- a tanner. And it sucked in different kind of chemicals. So it was a little bit warm. So he let me in at night to sleep over there. During the day, I was in the attic. And during the night, it's cold. It was December. Christmas is very cold in Ukraine, Russia. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- we-- eh, telephone's-- and-- and-- and he let-- he let me down then. It's all-- he came-- see, my mother came up to visit me dressed up, already warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Like ?] in the evenings, [? with ?] [? mine. ?] And she-- and she told me-- I'm-- now we have a place, where to stay. I'm staying with Rachel. My-- my-- that was the only place I was hoping to-- to get in. I came in to my-- to my next neighbor, where I was, near my house. And he says you can stay with me for a while. His name was Joseph Chaichuk. Childless, didn't have children. And he was working in the-- in the [? cohorts. ?] You know, they have their collective. They have a collective. He was the-- the guy what that kept the grains under lock. So we had plenty food because we had grains. And the house was a warm house. And she told me that. I was so happy I saw her. And I-- and we embraced and chatter. And I says, no. So far we have-- you have a place and we have a place. The winter, we'll be safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there she had-- she was staying by that guy, by that Mr. Joseph Chaichuk, with his wife, and food, and warm. I was staying there by my-- by my-- with that friend, with another one, six months. After six months, the Germans was desperate for working people. So we start mobilizing the Ukrainian youth to Germany, and send them back to Germany. So his daughter was my age. They was looking for his daughter. And she didn't want to go. She hide herself, too. So he came up to me. He says, listen, I cannot keep you here. They're going to find you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're going to kill me. They're going to kill you. And then we're going to be both in trouble. We have to go. My mother came up with me. And the only place I went is to that Ivan Chaichuk. And then others, in a different village. They called it [INAUDIBLE]. It's a small village, a little small one. Smaller than the village. Yeah, I came up to that guy. He was a poor guy with five children. My mother-- he says, I'm not-- I'm not-- then I-- I'm going to keep her as long as I can. And my mother left me there. But it-- over there. The first guy, what he say-- what did he said, Ivan Chaichuk. What he saved us with the food, with a-- with a place. And I was staying there a year till the liberation. He was a such a perfect, perfect, wonderful person, a pious man. A very good-natured man and understandable man. Then they had partisans-- a lot of partisans. They used to come in, take food by him, and things. So he used to  say to me-- I used to be-- I was a very good-looking girl when I was young. Young, good-looking woman. He says, you cannot be outside. You have to hide yourself. I don't want you to stay outside. You-- you have be hiding. Because I used to-- he put me under his children. The children used to sleep in the front, and I used to lay in the bed. And covered me up with that, like a little blanket. The partisan, the-- the young guys shouldn't see me. Such a good guy, he was. Very good, understandable man, person. And this way I was staying there the year, until liberation, until the December of '44. Every day, food. he had plenty-- I had plenty scares. They used-- Germans used to come in and look for food, for-- for partisans. They used to look around there all over. A couple times he were scared. I thought they're looking for me. But it came out it was not-- they didn't look for me. They looked for partisans there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe every day was a different scare. Every day, a different-- I never was in the house. I was outside, either in the bushes outside, either under the house, and the-- and the attic during the day. I was afraid to stay for the-- before the liberation. It was kind of bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a younger son, one son, two sons and three daughters, little ones, little kids. The son was 13, the younger one. The older one was 16, 15 or 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: The older one was really bad. Teenager, you know, he was. He used to say to his father-- to his mother, not to the father-- are you keeping this Jewish Jew? The [INAUDIBLE]? You don't need her. What do you need her for? But the younger guy was a very, very nice person. 13 years old, what did he know? He used to go in the woods. And he used to collect some berries and bring me, and-- and give me some berries, and talk to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was already-- they knew that the Russians are coming. Because it's already was '44. And Stalingrad was already taken by the Russians. They-- they used to say to me, don't worry. You're going to live through the war. And you'll go to America. And a lot of Jews are there. And you're going to have a good life. A young boy-- a young, little boy from 13 years, from the Ukraine, should tell me this thing. I was kind of wondering. So that was the story, until the-- and then the-- the front came through. There was fight-- fighting at night, all night. We had to go out-- and the outside from the house was-- so we heard a-- a lot of bombing. And the Germans didn't want to give up so easy. They had to take away their soldiers, bombing in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So was was in ditches. We was laying in the ditches, but a week time. It was-- and they burned the-- the village. There was a lot of fire there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the-- the Russian army came in. They established, right away, a hospital with a-- Hospital, well, they-- in the city, in the-- in the village. All the houses, what with-- with injured people, all of them, the soldiers. And they had some doctors, and nurses. And I used-- and I worked for the hospital. I helped out. It was already liberated. It was the end of '44. And worked and helped out the Russian people. And it was winter, wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where can you go, wintertime? Wintertime is hard to go. I didn't have no clothing, nothing, no shoes, nothing. Barefooted, I was going with-- with wooden shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed-- we stayed till May. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I worked there by the-- in the-- in the-- in the hospital there. They used to bring in a lot of injured-- injured soldiers. And they cured them there. Worked there. My sister was too young. But she was-- she was-- helped out too. And my mother was working-- working there, cleaning, washing, cooking something for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: One time she said to the guy, the Mr. Joseph Chaichuk, she says, there was Jewish people in the army. It was in the-- not in the front, but in the back. And she told him that the guy saved my life, what-- what shall I do? Can I-- can I pay him back? What-- what can I do? She said-- they said, they're going to mobilize them. If you wants to go with us, it's going to be in the back, not on the front. This way they put him, right away, on the front. Who knows if he's going to be alive? She said to him, Joseph, go with these people. They'll-- they'll-- you-- you'll go to the army. He was a young man still. They'll mobilize you. You have to-- you have go through. Who knows if you're going to be alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I don't want to go by myself. I'll go with my-- with my boys, from this-- from that-- from the village. I don't know. I don't want to go by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they mobilized him. And right away, maybe 30 miles from that village, they killed him, on the front. The Germans killed him. So we was left there with all the women. And we were staying with-- there up to the-- up to the end of the war. Not to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: May. May the 9th, it was liberation. We-- we were staying before. We went-- beginning of May, we went back to Korzec. My husband was, already, liberated from December. And he was staying in Korzec. He was not a husband yet. He was my-- my friend, boyfriend. Not a boyfriend either. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came there, to the town. First, I came back with my sister. We took-- we came back. It's about 30 miles around it. But it took us, maybe, more than 30 miles, 60 miles. Because we went-- we didn't want to go to the villages. We went [? through ?] the [? freeways. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took us about 60 miles. And we came back there. It was an-- and a warm day. And we came over there. They saw us and says, where was you up to now? We are already liberated for-- for a couple months, that we couldn't come [? over. ?] We was-- no, he was very happy to see me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my sister, talking. Just right away, he-- he was sleeping. And we had to [? sleep ?] [INAUDIBLE]. So he said, you can sleep in my bed and I'm going to go to a neighbor. Stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he was very good. And then he said, while you're staying there bring your mother here. [? Where ?] are you supposed to stay, in a village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went back. And we brought in the-- my mother there. And we got a-- a room, not an apartment, a room. Only one room. And the three of us was three beds, we didn't-- until we collected some bed to sleep, we didn't have a bed. We didn't have nothing. Nothing. Had to leave-- start from scratch. So I made myself-- my-- a-- a mattress from-- from sacks. You put it in, sew together. You put in some straw. That was the mattress. No-- no sheets, no nothing. No pillow. And we-- and we-- we-- we-- we got some people to give us some blankets. Neighbors gave us some blankets. And we organized. We had no-- no table, no food, no nothing. And then we organized ourselves. And we were starting-- and I start work for the Russians, the second time. I've worked in a office, as a bookkeeper-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --over there. And I worked in a office as a bookkeeper from-- from-- from a restaurant and from a dairy, different kind of offices. I worked in there because they mobilized people to-- to work. The Russians mobilized. They didn't have enough people to work in the-- in the coal mines. So they start mobilizing people to work in the coal mines. In the coal mines. See, I was a young girl. I didn't know what mean, coal mines. They want to take me to coal mines. If I'm working, they wouldn't take me out of work. My sister didn't. At night, they used to come in and take people. They were then taking young people to the-- to the coal mines. So at night I didn't have to-- I couldn't sleep at home at that time, too. I used to go someplace in the attics to sleep, not in the house. Because they-- they didn't-- they didn't ask you. They was looking for young people to-- to work. They didn't ask if you work, if you don't work. They take you. They grab you and they take you. The undercover, the KGB. We lived-- we were lived there until the-- till the end of the war, May 9th. But before that, we had our chance to register our self to go to Poland. Because we were-- we are-- [? was ?] to Poland, Polish citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they give us a-- a break. We can go out to Poland. So I was working in an office there, as a bookkeeper. They said-- the girls used to say, where are you going to go? What are you going to go there? Why don't you stay here, better? I says, I-- I don't want to-- I want to go out. So [? of that ?] [? either, ?] I wasn't afraid. I want to go out. I want to go to Israel, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, you're going to be suffering. The girls used to tell me, you'll be suffering. Stay here. You're here. They liked me there. I said, no. I registered myself. And then before the-- the end of the war. It was, I think, a week before. We had to go out from our town, leave the town, to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we grabbed our packages and whatever we had, a few things. And Nathan was-- my future husband-- was working in a factory, in a sugar-- sugar farm factory. So he had a machine. They took us by machine to nearest town, to the station, to the railroad station. Because our town didn't have us a railroad station. Over there, the railroad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we put our packages on the-- on the outside. And waiting for-- for-- to go. Because we didn't never-- no tickets. You cannot buy a ticket. They give you a car-- not a regular, like, a passenger car. They give you a car-- eh, a freight car. They seat you, like, in a freight, not in a passenger car. We wait for that, there, until they're going to give it to you. We was afraid, maybe, they're going to send us to Siberia. Well, finally, it was rain-- it was raining. You know, over there, in springtime, rains a lot. I had an uncle, a Russian. You know, he came from-- came back from Russia, my mother's uncle. It was my grand uncle. He had a house. So he said, you can stay for awhile, by-- by me. They didn't have the chance-- a chance to go out because of the Russian citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't let him out. And he says, to my mother, same thing. Where are you going to go? You're going to starve from hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother says, the First World War, I didn't go out. No, I don't want to see this-- this-- this land here. This-- this land is full of blood. I wanna run away with my kids. I have a chance. I'm going out. So she was-- she was very-- a smart woman. This way, she was very smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we late for this, for the-- for this-- for the train. And we came back to-- we came to Lodz. It took us maybe a week or two weeks to go there, until we came to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they brought us over in a German town. Then we transport back to Lodz. We didn't want to stay there long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came back with the-- when we came back to Lodz, the ghetto, Lodz, from ghetto, was still-- still was-- there was no Ghetto. But there was-- we saw the place where the Jewish used to stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Broken. Everything was-- you know furniture is out. Windows broken. We went and we looked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went there and we looked for our apartment. We find one room, a-- a-- a four-room apartment. So what do you need, four rooms? I didn't have furnitures. What am I going to do with four rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] You need only a bed to sleep. That's all. We went in, in one room. And we had another woman with us. She says, I don't want to stay by myself in a-- in a room. I want to sleep with you. So she had a place. So it was three women-- four women and one man. Nathan was the only man. [LAUGHS] Sleeping each-- each-- everybody had a bed. That's all. That's what we need. A table, we didn't have. There was folding bed, but that-- during the day it was a table. At night, was a bed. So then we start to live-- live-- staying there, and collecting food, having food. And the-- the Jewish Agency helped us out. There was a forming, right away, Jewish-- Jewish distributing-- joint-- Jewish distributing-- distributing agency sent from the United States. They send a lot of food for us, you know, canned food. and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to-- they used to give us the food, clothing. They used to send in food in Poland. And we were staying there till December, '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: December, and it is November, raining. November, they was-- they was-- start to kill Jews in Poland, around the small towns. The AK, the Polish AK, that was the national party there. They killed Jews a  lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we talked over with my mother. Nathan says, we have to run away from here. We can get killed here, too. Let's run. People are running to Berlin, to Germany, to West Germany. But before, he proposed to me. He wants to marry me. So I'm not going to go out like that. I want to get married. So we got married in a Jewish wedding. 19-- November 21, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, the next day, we went-- like he told, we went with a-- we went to-- to a town over there, and a German town, Szczecin, they called this town. Over there was-- they said over there, there are Russian soldiers. They're taking you over to Berlin. And not legally. It's not legally, like that. You pay money and they take you there at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went in there, and I find the guy. And a-- a grocer there. There was a grocery. And we find the Russian guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we talk to him. He says, yes, I'll take you. He gives me-- that guy gives me a lot of people. I-- I'm going to take about four of you. I'll take you over to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how much is it? Just 4,000 marks or zloties. [? You know, their ?] [? pile, ?] [? their money. ?] [? There ?] was nothing worth this money. So that night, 12 o'clock, it was right. We came up there. And they took us to Berlin. And the way we entered through, there were a lot of Polish-- Polish guard and-- and-- and German guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we passed through. We covered our self in blankets and we passed through. Some of them, they-- they-- took out, they caught. And they took them back. But not-- Luckily, we went through. And he brought us our self out there in Berlin, and the main street, Potsdamer Platz, that was the Russian zone. Busy in the-- and the radios are-- are, you know, playing in Russian, everything in Russian. Lenin, Stalin, there on the-- and so light. And he says-- he comes up, this Rafael, that guy, the Russian guy, he says, you have to go down. That's it. No more. You have to go down. We have to go down. And I said, let-- let's take a look, where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went-- Nathan went down and took a look. He says, we found a ditch there, a little thing. He said, let's go. We go out [? the door. ?] We paid him the money, 4,000 rubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we thought it's 16,000, but it was 4,000. And we went down in the ditch. It was 12, 1 o'clock at night, we're waiting there. Then we see people coming our from there. It was a sub-- a subway. A broken subway, but it was a subway. So we got up. And we didn't sleep all night anyways. Got up and was waiting. Then later on we find out where to go to the Jewish society, the Jewish-- he said in the Jewish [GERMAN], they called it in German, the Jewish [GERMAN]. That was in the-- I think it was in the-- in the English zone. We came up there with a-- with a car. They said, we are full. We cannot not take you in. Full. We have a lot of people here. We don't have a bed for you. You have to go in the Russian zone, well, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us with them, another-- another machine to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And there we was one room, we was-- maybe that was a three-- three-story beds.

speaker: interviewee
text: One bed and another bed. Just like military bed. You know, the bunks, like, wooden bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have no choice. We-- they give you like, a mattress and a little pillow. There was a pillow ready with a little blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're sitting on there. And they give you some food over there. The Jewish organization provide you with food. It came in in December, the end of December, in 1945, over there. And maybe it was in the middle of December. I think it's in the middle of December. Because later on, the Germans, they-- we finded out that they want to round us up and send-- the Russians, they want to send us back to Russia, you know, or to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We packed our self. And we had to take the elevator up to nine o'clock in the evening. But later on, does-- the elevator doesn't go. We packed ourselves. We run away to the French zone. 9 o'clock-- we made it. We came to the French zone and find the place there, where the Jewish people were saying there. And we were staying there until beginning of 1946. And they round-- rounded up us too. They want to-- they says, there's no place here. You have to go someplace else. There's too many people. You have to divide yourself. We didn't want to go out. So they round us up. Everybody was thinking they taking a bad, someplace. They-- you know, everybody was scared. I was jumping through the windows from the second floor. They came up. Somebody had called up the American-- you know, the American people, the American soldiers. And that's-- they are rounding up. We are Jewish refugees. And they want to take us. We don't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So came in a rabbi. You know, a rabbi from the-- from the Army. And he said-- and he spoke in Jewish, even to us. He was speaking Jewish. And he says, what do they want from me? And made pictures and so-- pictures and he said, he was talking to the French. And the French finally decided that they're not taking-- they don't take us. Let them stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and the American rabbi took the-- all the men. They made a minyan and they start davening. It was happy. Let's all give some. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're standing-- we were staying there for a while, not too long. And then they organized a-- a campaign that was in a-- a-- a German-- a German military base there. So they made it for a DP camp, from-- for refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the American zone, [GERMAN]. So we went there. Then, right away, they give us a room. And the-- everything was organized there. And we had a room by ourselves, and my mother and my sister, separately. We was staying there for a while, till-- till March. And then we moved to West Germany, Eschwege. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pick up in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: That--
